Google 


This  is  a  digital  copy  of  a  book  that  was  preserved  for  generations  on  library  shelves  before  it  was  carefully  scanned  by  Google  as  part  of  a  project 

to  make  the  world's  books  discoverable  online. 

It  has  survived  long  enough  for  the  copyright  to  expire  and  the  book  to  enter  the  public  domain.  A  public  domain  book  is  one  that  was  never  subject 

to  copyright  or  whose  legal  copyright  term  has  expired.  Whether  a  book  is  in  the  public  domain  may  vary  country  to  country.  Public  domain  books 

are  our  gateways  to  the  past,  representing  a  wealth  of  history,  culture  and  knowledge  that's  often  difficult  to  discover. 

Marks,  notations  and  other  maiginalia  present  in  the  original  volume  will  appear  in  this  file  -  a  reminder  of  this  book's  long  journey  from  the 

publisher  to  a  library  and  finally  to  you. 

Usage  guidelines 

Google  is  proud  to  partner  with  libraries  to  digitize  public  domain  materials  and  make  them  widely  accessible.  Public  domain  books  belong  to  the 
public  and  we  are  merely  their  custodians.  Nevertheless,  this  work  is  expensive,  so  in  order  to  keep  providing  tliis  resource,  we  liave  taken  steps  to 
prevent  abuse  by  commercial  parties,  including  placing  technical  restrictions  on  automated  querying. 
We  also  ask  that  you: 

+  Make  non-commercial  use  of  the  files  We  designed  Google  Book  Search  for  use  by  individuals,  and  we  request  that  you  use  these  files  for 
personal,  non-commercial  purposes. 

+  Refrain  fivm  automated  querying  Do  not  send  automated  queries  of  any  sort  to  Google's  system:  If  you  are  conducting  research  on  machine 
translation,  optical  character  recognition  or  other  areas  where  access  to  a  large  amount  of  text  is  helpful,  please  contact  us.  We  encourage  the 
use  of  public  domain  materials  for  these  purposes  and  may  be  able  to  help. 

+  Maintain  attributionTht  GoogXt  "watermark"  you  see  on  each  file  is  essential  for  in  forming  people  about  this  project  and  helping  them  find 
additional  materials  through  Google  Book  Search.  Please  do  not  remove  it. 

+  Keep  it  legal  Whatever  your  use,  remember  that  you  are  responsible  for  ensuring  that  what  you  are  doing  is  legal.  Do  not  assume  that  just 
because  we  believe  a  book  is  in  the  public  domain  for  users  in  the  United  States,  that  the  work  is  also  in  the  public  domain  for  users  in  other 
countries.  Whether  a  book  is  still  in  copyright  varies  from  country  to  country,  and  we  can't  offer  guidance  on  whether  any  specific  use  of 
any  specific  book  is  allowed.  Please  do  not  assume  that  a  book's  appearance  in  Google  Book  Search  means  it  can  be  used  in  any  manner 
anywhere  in  the  world.  Copyright  infringement  liabili^  can  be  quite  severe. 

About  Google  Book  Search 

Google's  mission  is  to  organize  the  world's  information  and  to  make  it  universally  accessible  and  useful.   Google  Book  Search  helps  readers 
discover  the  world's  books  while  helping  authors  and  publishers  reach  new  audiences.  You  can  search  through  the  full  text  of  this  book  on  the  web

BOOK   PRODUCTION   IN    1901 


BOOK  PRODUCTION  IN  1901 


IN  THE  UNITED  STATES. 


The  opening  year  of  the  twentieth  century 
was  inaugurated  in  its  spring  months  b"  a  per- 
fect flood  of  books,  that  increased  in  volume 
through  the  fall,  reaching  a  climax,  amazing 
in  numbers  during  the  holiday  season.  The 
rear  1901  can  scarcely  be  considered  notable 
for  the  number  of  itt  important  original 
works,  though  there  were  abundance  of  ex- 
cellent and  even  seemingly  permanent  addi- 
tions to  every  class  of  literature.  It  was 
rather  a  year  conspicuous  for  the  making  of 
books,  than  the  publishing  of  books — if  we 
may  make  that  difference — a  year  of  new  edi- 
tions of  standard  works  or  finely  illustrated 
editions  of  favorites  of  former  years,  as  well 
as  of  low  priced  and  popular  editions  of  books 
or  sets  of  books  always  in  demand.  Added 
to  the  constant  stream  of  new  novels  coming 
from  leading  publishers  and  authors,  the 
market  was  crowded  by  the  manufacturers 
of  books  with  new  issues  of  standard  English 
and  American  novels,  that  had  outlived  their 
copyright  limitations,  and  cheap  paper  copies 
of  a  poorer  class  of  fiction,  that  induded  a 
great  deal  of  utterly  worthless  material.  In 
spite  of  the  admitted  rubbish  in  the  count  of 
fiction,  the  literary  reputation  of  the  year 
rested  largely  upon  its  novels — and  its  bio- 
graphical works.  While  many  of  the  novels 
promise  to  be  less  short-lived  than  others, 
and  hold  forth  the  expectation  of  perhaps 
being  included  at  a  future  day  among  "the 
books  for  all  time" — the  biographies,  if  less 
in  number,  may  be  spoken  of  with  more  con- 
fidence. They  belong  to  the  most  delightful 
and  instructive  class  of  literature.  Few  of 
them  but  are  valuable  and  interesting,  a  large 
number  being  acknowledged  as  authoritative 
and  definitive. 

Table  No.  I  which  fallows  gives  in  classes 
the  figures,  approximately,  of  the  book  pro- 
dnction  in  this  country  in  igoi,  with  those  of 
igoo  for  comparison.  Almost  all  of  the  de- 
partments show  an  increase — all  naturally 
being  influenced  by  the  great  activity  of  the 
year.  There  is  a  slight  ^ling  off  in  numbers, 
from  the  previous  year  in  the  classes  of  Edu- 
cation, Law,  and  Political  and  Social  Science. 
but  all  others  are  far  in  advance  in  their  to- 
tals, of  any  other  publishing  year.  Notably 
so  are  the  claMes  of  Fiction,  Literature  and 


Cdlectcd   Works,   Juvenile   Fiction,    Poetry, 
and  Biography.

BOOK  PRODUCTION  IN  1901. 


Table  No.  2  attempts  to  show  the  num- 
ber of  books  manufactured  in  the  United 
States  in  1901 — first  those  by  American  au- 
thors, second  those  by  English  or  other  for- 
eign authors  made  in  this  country  accord- 
ing to  the  demands  of  copyright  law,  and 
third  the  books  in  English  imported  bound  or 
in  sheets;  these  three  classes  comprising  the 
book  production  of  the  United  States  in  1901. 
The  first  and  second  classes  are  almost  all 
copyright  books — though  strange  to  say,  not 
all  books  made  in  this  country,  whether  from 


American  or  foreign  author,  are  invariably 
copyrighted.  This  table  like  the  former, 
scarcely  claims  to  be  exactly  correct,  as  it  is 
impossible  always  to  trace  the  history  of  a 
work,  from  its  author  to  its  final  publication. 
The  table  shows  4701  books  by  American  au- 
thors against  3626  of  1900;  2122  books  by 
English  or  other  foreign  authors  (made  here) 
against  1388  of  1900;  and  1318  books,  or 
sheets  imported,  against  1124  of  1900.  The 
reprints  were  as  usual  the  largest  in  Fiction, 
amounting  to  1.028  books. 


REPORT  OF  THE  REGISTER  OF  COPYRIGHTS  FOR  1900-1901. 


The  report  of  Thorvald  Solberg,  Register 
of  Copyrights,  for  the  fiscal  year  ending  June 
30,  1901,  appears  as  an  appendix  to  the  report 
of  the  Librarian  of  Congress,  just  issued,  for 
the  same  period.  It  presents  the  following 
facts : 

COPYRIGHT    ENTRIES. 

The  total  number  of  entries  of  titles  dur- 
ing the  fiscal  year  was  92,351.  Of  this  num- 
ber 83,813  were  titles  of  productions  of  per- 
sons citizens  or  residents  of  the  United  States, 
and  8538  were  titles  of  productions  of  per- 
sons not  citizens  or  residents  of  the  United 
States.  The  fees  for  these  entries  were: 
United  States,  $41,906.50;  foreign,  $8538,  or 
a  total  of  $50,444.50. 

Of  the  foreign  entries,  1995  were  with  cer- 
tificates, as  well  as  21,810  of  the  United  States 
entries,  or  a  total  of  23,805  certificates,  at  fees 
amounting  to  $11,902.50.  In  addition,  1334 
copies  of  record  were  furnished  at  fees 
amounting  to  $667;  550  assignments  were 
recorded  and  certified  at  a  charge  of  $641,  and 
search  fees  charged  to  the  amount  of  $32.50. 

With  the  beginning  of  the  new  century  the 
record  books  were  divided  into  nine  separate 
series,  to  correspond  with  the  nine  classes  of 
articles  named  in  the  copyright  law — a  plan 
that  has  resulted  in  more  systematic  and  effec- 
tive record,  and  that  supplies,  at  the  same 
time,  an  automatic  classification  and  enumera- 
tion of  entries. 

COPYRIGHT  DEPOSITS. 

The  various  articles  deposited  in  compli- 
ance with  the  copyright  law,  which  have  been 
receipted  for,  stamped,  credited,  indexed,  and 
catalogued  during  the  fiscal  year  amount  to 
162,283.  This  is  a  gain  of  20,839  over  the  pre- 
vious fiscal  year. 

There  has  been  a  steady  growth  in  the  num- 
ber of  these  deposits  during  the  last  four 
fiscal  years,  the  total  deposits  being  in  each 
year  respectively,  112,805;  120,143;  141.444 
«nd  i6a,a83. 


Comparing  the  deposits  with  the  entries  it 
is  found  that  only  in  the  case  of  periodicals 
is  the  deposit  complete,  although  in  music 
it  is  substantially  so,  the  deposits  amounting 
to  about  99  per  cent,  of  the  entries,  while 
in  the  case  of  the  various  entries  necessarily 
classified  under  the  term  "book,"  only  about 
70  per  cent,  are  completed  by  deposit  as  re- 
quired by  law — a  large  proportion  of  the  en- 
tries probably  being  for  projected  works  pro- 
duced later  or  never  completed  at  all — and 

in  the  case  of  the  remaining  articles  about  80 
per  cent,  of  the  entries  are  perfected  by  de- 
posits. 

These  deposits  for  the  four  years  are  clas- 
sified as  follows :

BOOK  PRODUCTION  IN  igoi. 


IX 


COPYRIGHT  CATALOGUE  AND  INDEX. 

The  technical  record  work  of  the  office  is 
summarized.  All  titles  filed  for  record  are 
carefully  indexed,  each  entry  having  a  card 
under  the  name  of  the  proprietor ;  and  books, 
periodicals,  dramatic  compositions,  and  maps 
have,  in  addition,  title  or  author  cards.  These 
index  cards,  numbering  115,025  for  the  fiscal 
year,  become  part  of  the  permanent  indexes 
of  the  Copyright  Office,  and  are  also  used  as 
the  copy  for  the  Catalogue  of  Title  Entries 
printed  weekly.  A  complete  volume  index  is 
now  supplied  with  the  catalogue,  which  it  is 
hoped  to  continue. 

"The  copyright  entries  from  July  10,  1870, 
to  .\ugust  31,  1901,  number  1,258,304.  The 
index  to  these  entries  consists  of  upward  of 
700,000  cards,  and  more  than  100,000  cards  are 
added  annually.  These  cards  index  the  en- 
tries primarily  under  the  names  of  the  pro- 
prietors of  the  copyrights,  and, it  is  believed 
that  this  index  of  proprietors  is  substantially 
complete  from  July,  1870,  so  that  under  the 
name  of  each  copyright  proprietor  there  is  a 
card  or  cards  showing  the  titles  of  all  arti- 
des  upon  which  copyright  is  claimed.  In  ad- 
dition to  cards  under  the  proprietors*  names, 
cards  are  now  made:  For  books,  under  the 
names  of  their  authors ;  for  anonymous  books, 
periodicals,  and  dramatic  compositions,  under 
the  first  words  of  the  titles  (not  a,  and,  or 
the),  and  for  maps,  under  the  leading  subject 
words  of  the  titles,  i.e.,  the  names  of  the 
localities  mapped. 

"No  attempt  is  made  to  index  the  titles  as 
soch ;  that  is  to  say.  in  order  to  show  that  any 
given  title  has  been  used.  So  long  as  the 
copyright  law  does  not  secure  the  use  of  a 
registered  title  to  some  one  person  to  the  ex- 
clusion of  all  others,  there  would  seem  to  be 
DO  justification  in  adding  to  the  already  large 
index,  upward  of  100.000  cards  annually,  sim- 
ply to  show  that  certain  forms  of  words  have 
been  used  by  one  or  more  persons  as  desig- 
nations for  books,  maps,  music,  photographs, 
etc.  registered  for  copyright  protection." 

COPYRIGHT  OFFICE   WORK. 

The  status  of  the  work  of  the  office  is  re- 
ported upon,  both  for  current  routine,  and 
arrears.  All  current  work  has  been  kept  well 
np  to  date  and  effective  headway  has  been 
made  toward  the  systematic  arrangement  of 
the  great  mass  of  material  accumulated  dur- 
ing the  years  prior  to  July  i.  1897. 


Appendixes  to  the  report  give  tabulated 
statistics  of  deposits,  expenses,  and  similar 
details. 

COPYRIGHT  LEGISLATION. 

In  conclusion  Mr.  Solberg  touches  sugges- 
tively upon  the  need  of  further  copvright 
legislation.     He  says: 

"I  have  frequently  had  occasion  to  call  at- 
tention to  the  need  for  new  copyright  legisla- 
tion. The  law  now  in  force  consists  of  the 
act  of  July  8,  1870,  as  edited  to  become  title 
60,  chapter  3  of  the  Revised  Statutes,  and 
ten  amendatory  acts  passed  subsequently. 
Naturally,  there  is  lacking  the  consistency 
and  homogeneity  of  a  single  well-considered 
copyright  statute.  The  existing  legislation  is 
antiquated:  inadequate  in  some  directions, 
inconsistent  and  confusing  in  others.  The 
Attorney-General  of  the  United  States,  in  a 
recent  opinion  concerning  some  provisions  of 
the  copyright  law,  after  setting  out  the  pre- 
cise stipulations  of  the  statutes  relating  there- 
to, says:  *Under  this  kind  of  legislation  it  is 
impossible  to  arrive  at  any  satisfactory  con- 
clusion as  to  what  Congress  really  did  intend 
by  it.'  The  expression  of  such  an  opinion 
by  the  head  of  the  judicial  department  of  the 
Government  is  sufficient  evidence  that  re- 
vision of  the  law  is  urgently  required. 

"Our  copyright  laws  are  based  upon  an- 
tiquated models — the  early  English  copyright 
statutes — and  the  amendments  made  from 
time  to  time  to  the  original  enactment  have 
been  aimed  at  improvement  of  the  law  in 
certain  particulars  rather  than  a  thorough  re- 
vision, and  have  not  given  to  it  a  form  corre- 
sponding to  modern  ideas  of  legislation  relat- 
ing to  literary  and  artistic  property. 

"No  attempt  at  a  general  revision  of  the  law 
has  been  made  since  that  by  the  commis- 
sioners appointed  in  1868  to  revise  the  gen- 
eral statutes,  and  their  treatment  of  copyright 
was  necessarily  a  partial  one,  that  being  only 
one  of  a  great  many  subjects  requiring  con- 
sideration. Many  and  greatly  diverse  inter- 
ests are  affected  by  copyright  legislation,  and 
it  would  seem  more  probable  that  each  and  all 
of  these  would  receive  proportional  attention 
if  the  task  of  preparing  a  codification  of  the 
copyright  laws  were  intrusted  by  Congress  to 
a  commission  adequately  representing  the  dif- 
ferent interests  concerned,  and  that  a  project 
of  law  thus  formulated  would  more  likely  be 
on  a  par  with  the  existmg  progressive  copy- 
right legislation  of  other  countries." 


BOOK  PRODUCTION  IN  1901. 


IN  EUROPE. 


ESGLAND. 

The  following  annual  table,  exhibiting  the 
■imount  and  character  of  the  book  production 


of  Great  Britain  during  the  past  year,  pre- 
pared by  the  London  Publuhers'  Circular, 
shows  a  decrease  of  about  a  thousand  vol- 
umes from  the  total  of  1900.  This  decrease 
would  have  been  larger  but  for  the  great 


quantity  of  new  fiction  issued  during  the  last 
two  monthj  of  igoi.  In  igoo  new  noveli 
issued  in  November  and  December  numbered 
313  and  95 ;  in  igoi  they  numbered  372  and 
I3S,  respectively.  The  total  number  of  new 
novels  published  in  igoo  was  1563;  in  igoi 
it  was  1513,  only  fifty  fewer.  Poetry  shows 
a  fall  of  more  than  a  hundred.  Of  new 
works  on  voyages  and  travels  the  number  is 
precisely  the  same  as  in  the  previous  year, 
but  in  reprints  there  is  a  fall  of  forty.  In 
1900  history  and  biography  (including  South 
African  events,  territories,  races  and  nota- 
bilities) composed  one  of  the  few  classes 
which  showed  an  increase ;  now  it  shows  a 
decrease  of  nearly  two  hundred. 

FKA  ffCE. 

The  Bibliographie  dt  la  France,  in  its  issue 
for  December  28,  igoi,  gives  the  following 
totals  of  the  number  of  books,  musical  com- 
positions, engravings,  etc.,  published  in 
France  during  1901.  The  number  of  books 
recorded  was  13.053,  or  309  books  less  than 
in  1900.  The  number  of  musical  composi- 
tions recorded  was  6550,  showing  an  increase 
of  640  over  those  of  1900.  The  number  of 
engravings,  lithographs  and  photographs  was 
TJ&,  a  decrease  of  174  over  those  recorded  in 
1900.

ANNUAL   AMERICAN    CATALOGUE 

Cumulated  1900-1901 

By  Author^  litle^  Subject  and  Series  Entry 


NOTE. 

In  this  list  tfu  books  mre  recorded  under  (i)  author^  in  Olarondon  type;  anonymous  books  kaving^ 
01ar«iidon  type  for  the  first  word;  (2)  title  in  Roman  ;  (3)  subject-heading  in  small  caps  ;  (4)  name 
of  series  in  Italics,  The  figures  in  parentheses  are  not  the  imprint  date^  but  refer  to  the  date  of 
••  The  Publishers'  Weekly  "  in  which  full  title  entry  will  be  found  and  not  to  the  day  of  publication^ 
for  which  information  should  be  sought  in  the  full  title  entry  thus  indicated.  Where  not  specified, 
the  binding  is  cloth. 

The  abbreviations  are  usually  self-explanatory,     A  colon  after  initial  designates  the  most  usual 
given  name t  as:  A:  Augustus ;  B:  Benjamin;  C:  Charles;  D:  David;  E:  Edward;  F:  Frederic; 
G:  George ;  H:    Henry ;  I:  Isaac ;  J:  John ;  L:  Louis  ;  N:  Nicholas ;   P:  Peter ;  R:  Richard; 
S:  Samuel;  T:  Thomas;   W:  William, 

Sites  are  designated  as  follows  :  F,  {folio  :  over  30  centimeters  high);  Q,  {^0 :  under  30  cm,); 
O,  {J&vo:  25  cm,);  D,  {i2mo:  20  cm,);  S,  (i6mo:  17%  cm,);  T,  (24010.*  15  cm,);  Tt.  {32mo:  12^  cm,); 
Fe,  (4Smo:  10  cm.),     Sq,,  obi,,  nar.,  designate  square,  oblong,  narrow  books  of  these  heights. 

Months  are  abbreviated  as  follows  :  fa  January  ;  F  February  ;  Mr  March  ;  Ap  April ;  My  May  ; 
Jc  June ;  Jl July ;  Ag  August;  S  September  ;  O  October ;  N November  ;  D  December. 

The  full  name  of  publisher  and  address  will  be  found  in  the  Directory  of  American  Publishertm 
pp.  xiii^xxxix.

THE  AMERICAN  CATALOGUE  of  books  in 
print  and  for  sale  July  i,  1876,  compiled  under  the 
direction  of  F.  Lrypoldt,  ana  its  supplements,  1876- 
84, 188^-00,  i89(>-95,  and  1^5-1000,  compiled  under  the 
editorial  direction  of  R.  R.  Bowker,  aims  to  present 
all  the  bibliographical  features  of  the  books  in  the 
American  market,  arranged  in  the  6rstpart  alphabet- 
ically by  both  author*  and  titles^  and  in  the  second 
part  alphabetically  by  snSjttts. 

The  Catalogue  and  its  supplementary  volumes  form 
the  only  approximatelv  complete  guide  in  existence  to 
the  American  books  of  the  day, so  arranged  as  to  make 
reference  easy  from  whatever  direction  the  inquiry 
may  come,  whether  from  that  of  the  author,  or  the 
title,  or  the  subject.  It  not  only  furnishes  the  desired 
information  about  any  particular  book  of  which  the 
consulter  is  in  search,  but  shows  what  others  there 
are  by  the  same  author  or  on  the  same  subject  in  which 
he  is  interested.  To  the  bookseller,  therefore,  it  is 
valuable  both  in  filling  orders  and  in  stimulating  busi- 
ness; to  the  librarian,  in  supplying  gaps  and  propor- 
tioning his  collection;  and  to  all  who  are  practically 
concerned  with  baoks.in  furnishinginformation  which 
nowhere  else  is  obtainable  by  so  convenient  a  method, 
if  obuinableatall.

may  be  had  in  half  leather  binding  at  9(5-

'TPO  meet  a  wider  demand  than  has  shown  itself  for  ''The  Annual 
-*-       American  Catalogue,"  it   is   proposed    to   replace   that  publi- 
cation this  year  with 

The  Annual 

American  Catalogue 

Cumulated,   1900-1901 

mcluding  in  one  alphabet  the  short  title  lists  of  the  books  of  1900  and 
1 90 1,  giving  the  information  by  author,  title,  subject  and  series.  This 
cumulation  is  to  continue  annually  until  a  five-yearly  volume  of 
"  The  American  Catalogue "  in  its  new  shape  is  reached.  It  is 
hoped  that  this  form  of  catalogue  will  prove  a  desirable,  practical 
working  tool  for  the  trade,  supplementing  as  it  will  do,  from  year  to 
year,  the  last  issue  of  the  five-yearly  *'  American  Catalogue,"  without 
the  need  of  consulting  several  alphabets  in  several  volumes. 

8vo,  cloth,     .     -     -     $2.00 

if  ordered  and  paid  for  in  advance. 

Should  there  be  enough  demand  for  the  "Annual  Catalogue  "  as 

heretofore  published,  with  full   titles  and    annotations    of   the   books 

for  1901,  bound  in  half  leather,  at  $3.50,  or  in  sheets  at  $3.00,  we 
should  be  prepared  also  to  continue  that  publication. 

The  Annual  American  Cata-logue,  Cumulated,  1900-1901, 
which  has  been  unavoidably  delayed,  will  be  ready  this  month. 
Orders,  specifying  in  which  form  the  Catalogue  is  desired,  should 
be  addressed  at  an  early  date  to 

The  Office  OF  THE  PUBLISHERS'  WEEKLY 

298  Broadway  (P.  O.  Box  943),  New  York 

J^^The   Annual   English  Catalogue,  for  which  we  have  the  American 
market,  will  be  furnished  at  $1.50  in  paper,  or  bound  with  the  Annual  American 
Catalogue,  Cumulated,  1900-1901,  in  cloth,  at  $3.75  if  ordered  and  paid  for  in 
advance.
